title: Crosstalk In Contemporary Quantum Devices
authors: Spiro Gicev, Ben Harper, Haiyue Kang, Muhammad Usman, Martin Sevior
abstract: Crosstalk noise derives from phenomena in quantum devices which inhibit individual addressability or cause unintended interactions among qubits. It is widely considered one of the major problems to be solved for a quantum computing platform to operate at scales beyond one or two qubits. Despite this, detailed discussion of crosstalk is often neglected when quantum device performance is described both in the context of device benchmarking and individual algorithm execution. Additionally, while the potential for crosstalk exists in all quantum platforms, the mechanisms and severity of crosstalk between platforms varies significantly, increasing the barrier of entry associated with understanding and performing research on unfamiliar quantum platforms. While previous work focused on theoretical formalism or platform specific details, in this review article, we provide a comprehensive overview of crosstalk from quantum computing literature across a range of physical systems focusing on physical origins, methods of mitigation and known consequential security vulnerabilities. We describe multiple crosstalk mechanisms for all major quantum computing platforms, which are usually implicitly addressed through device design, tuning, and mitigation techniques. We also observe accelerating research regarding security implications, however with multiple avenues for further exploration, especially for non-superconducting systems. Together, this review provides a comprehensive entry point for researchers and industry engineers interested in understanding and addressing the challenges arising from crosstalk phenomena in modern quantum computing systems.
summary: This review article provides a comprehensive overview of crosstalk phenomena across all major quantum computing platforms. It identifies the physical origins of unintended interactions, evaluates current mitigation strategies, and highlights the emerging security risks associated with crosstalk in shared quantum environments.
main_problem: Crosstalk in quantum devices causes unintended interactions and lack of individual addressability, acting as a major barrier to scaling quantum computing platforms.
main_result: The paper provides a comprehensive review of crosstalk mechanisms, mitigation strategies, and security vulnerabilities across diverse quantum platforms, noting that structured noise becomes dominant as hardware improves.
method: The authors perform a comparative review of literature across multiple physical architectures, categorizing crosstalk by physical origin, diagnostic methods, and mitigation techniques.
model_or_system: The review covers a wide range of platforms including superconducting circuits, trapped ions, neutral atom arrays, photonic systems, semiconductor quantum dots, and NV centers in diamond.
key_observables: Gate fidelities, coherence times, error rates, Rabi ratios, correlated errors, and Pauli channel parameters.
important_parameters: Qubit number, gate times, frequency detuning, interatomic spacing, and photon loss.
main_assumptions: The review assumes that crosstalk is a primary limiting factor for the transition from NISQ-era devices to large-scale, fault-tolerant quantum computers.
figures_summary: Figure 1 provides a visual catalog of various quantum computing platforms and companies; Figure 2 illustrates specific crosstalk examples such as optical pulse spillover, thermal heating in photonics, and frequency crowding.
paper_structure: The paper defines the problem of crosstalk and its impact on scaling, provides a platform-by-platform review of physical origins, surveys mitigation techniques, discusses emerging security vulnerabilities, and identifies open research questions.
why_interesting: It is highly relevant for researchers in open quantum systems and quantum optics as it details how environmental and control-induced interactions (like ZZ coupling or photon spillover) lead to correlated noise and decoherence in multi-qubit registers.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Quantum computing platforms. a) The superconducting platform, which includes Google, IBM, Oxford Quantum Circuits, and Rigetti, represented by an IBM superconducting circuit [1]. b) The trapped ion platform, which includes IonQ and Quantinuum, represented by a schematic of a trapped ion array by IonQ [2]. c) The neutral atom platform, which includes Atom Computing, Infleqtion, Pasqal, Planqc, and QuEra, represented by an Infleqtion schematic [3]. d) The photonic platform, which includes PsiQuantum and Xanadu, represented by a PsiQuantum photonic circuit schematic [4]. e) The semiconductor platform, which includes Diraq, Intel, Silicon Quantum Computing, and QuTech, represented by a...

FIG. 2. Examples crosstalk phenomena in QPUs across different platforms. a) Always-on interactions causing oscillations in fidelity with dependence on neighbouring qubit states on an IBM QPU [27]. b) A diagram representing optical pulse spillover during MS gates in a system of trapped ions [28]. c) Two images showing a image of neutral atom readout with short (left) and long (right) exposure times [29]. d) Undesired heating of adjacent thermal phase-shifters in a photonic circuit [30]. e) Gate spillover for barrier and plunger drive in a QuTech quantum dot QPU [31]. f) Frequency crowding observed by nuclear spin spectroscopy in a QuTech nitrogen vacancy quantum device [32].

FIG. 3. Crosstalk mitigation techniques. a) A heavy-hexagon lattice with connectivity of degree 3, requires only three unique frequencies so adjacent qubits do not share a frequency, reducing frequency collisions as described in Ref. [22]. b) Simulated variation of the Z component of magnetic field strength for systems where control is performed with dual concentric loops [127]. c) ZZ interaction strength as a function of qubit-qubit detuning and bus frequency in a superconducting QPU with tunable-couplers [40]. d) Flux crosstalk between qubits and couplers before and after compensating for non-local flux when driving individual flux control lines in a superconducting QPU [51]. e)...

FIG. 4. Security vulnerabilities enabled by crosstalk in QPUs. a) An active attack, where a quantum computer tenant attacks a co-tenant using a sequence of repeated CNOT gates on superconducting hardware [147]. b) A passive attack, where always-on interactions cause a victim simulation to fail due to an attack simulation which was placed too close [67]. c) Spying, where crosstalk detection using an adversarial quantum circuit facilitates victim circuit identification, based on descriptions given in Ref. [154].
